question: According to the Camunda 8 best-practices guide, what are the two basic options for connecting the Zeebe workflow engine with your application or remote systems?

options.text: Write programming code using a client library for your language, or use an existing connector that just needs configuration.
Embed the Zeebe broker inside the application JVM, or connect to it over a JDBC driver.
Run the Camunda 7 process engine inside the application, or call Camunda 8 over SOAP.
Mount Zeebe's data folder as a shared volume, or poll the broker via REST every second.

a.text: The doc explicitly lists the two basic options: write programming code that leverages the client library for the language of choice, or use an existing connector that just needs configuration.

b.text: Zeebe is described as a remote system reached over gRPC or REST — not as an in-JVM embedded engine, and not via JDBC.

c.text: Camunda 8 connectivity is via the Zeebe client or a connector, not by embedding the legacy Camunda 7 engine or using SOAP.

d.text: Shared volumes and per-second REST polling are not the documented connectivity patterns — the client library hides the remote protocol.

explanation: The Camunda 8 best-practices page lays out exactly two options for hooking the workflow engine up to your applications: programming code that uses a Zeebe client library, or an existing connector configured for the integration. Everything else (REST, messaging, Kafka) sits underneath one of those two patterns.

docs.title: Connecting the workflow engine with your world
